text: The patient is positioned supine with legs spread, allowing the surgeon to stand between them. Three 12mm ports and two 5mm ports are inserted for the laparoscopic Whipple procedure, with the possibility of an additional port.

The surgeon divides the fat between the colon and the lower border of the stomach, identifying the pink vascular tissue of the stomach. The dissection continues to separate the colonic fat from the kidney fat, which is adherent to the liver in this slightly obese patient. The right colon is mobilized to clear the surgical field for subsequent steps.

The surgeon continues to divide the fat between the stomach and colon, gradually lifting the stomach away from the pancreas. A small window is opened to access the area behind the stomach, with additional fat being divided as it extends over the duodenum.

The surgeon dissects the fatty tissue surrounding veins draining from the stomach and a small artery running to the stomach. A tunnel is created underneath the stomach, revealing the liver behind it. A prominent lymph node is visible over the hepatic artery, and the pancreas is exposed as a pink structure behind the fat. The dissection continues to remove the fatty leash containing the stomach vessels.

The surgeon uses a stapler to transect the gastropoploic vessels as a whole, preparing for the division of the stomach. The stomach is then divided to facilitate better exposure of the pancreas for the resection of the duodenum.

The surgeon dissects around the prominent lymph node on the artery leading to the liver, carefully removing the node to expose the artery. The artery running down into the duodenum and pancreas is identified for division as part of the pancreaticoduodenectomy procedure.

The surgeon continues dissection across and in front of the duodenum to mobilize it from the porta hepatis, revealing the gastroduodenal artery that runs to the pancreas and duodenum, which needs to be divided. The hepatic artery is elevated with an instrument on the right side, while the portal vein is visible behind these structures, transporting blood from the bowel, pancreas, and spleen to the liver.
The surgeon performs further dissection, revealing the portal vein and the common bile duct, which are critical structures to consider during the pancreaticoduodenectomy.
The surgeon carefully teases the bile duct away from the portal vein, creating space to pass an instrument behind these structures. Following this separation, the bile duct is prepared for division.
The surgeon divides the connection between the gallbladder and the barlet, revealing the long cystic artery. A stapler is used to divide the barlet, preventing bleeding from the cut edge.
The surgeon operates below the pancreas to define a tunnel underneath the pancreatic neck, which runs over the superior mesenteric vein, preparing for the division of the pancreas.

The surgeon lifts the pancreatic neck forward and uses a harmonic scalpel to dissect and control small blood vessels. The superior mesenteric vein is visible, running beneath the pancreas and transitioning into the portal vein. The pancreas is carefully teased off the vein, creating a safe tunnel without branches running into the pancreas.

The surgeon slowly creates a large window underneath the pancreas, allowing an instrument to pass through to the opposite side. A tape is then passed around the neck of the pancreas, which will be used to lift the pancreas up and forward, providing tension on the tissues for further dissection alongside the vein.

The surgeon opens a plane between the fat of the bowel, specifically the colon, and the head of the pancreas, including the uncinate process. This bloodless plane is adjacent to the vein, facilitating the separation of the colon from the pancreas.

The surgeon separates the duodenum from the colon by dissecting through a plane of areolar tissue, which appears bubbly and indicates a safe area without blood vessels. The duodenum is visible and grasped, while the colon's fat is teased down and away from the duodenum. An assistant uses force to retract the bowel, facilitating further dissection.
The surgeon performs the Cocker maneuver, rolling the duodenum away from the posterior abdominal structures. The inferior vena cava is visible as the operation proceeds in front of it.

The surgeon continues to elevate the duodenum from the fatty structures of the bowel and off the inferior vena cava, dividing the loose connective tissue attachments. The dissection is relatively bloodless due to the absence of blood vessels in this tissue.

The surgeon carefully pulls the duodenum from beneath the superior mesenteric artery and vein, exposing the surrounding fatty tissue. The aorta is visible, identified as the large artery supplying blood to the lower body. The dissection proceeds along the front of the inferior vena cava, with the liver visible in the background.

The surgeon dissects the left renal vein as it crosses the aorta, just below the origin of the superior mesenteric artery. The duodenum is pushed to the patient's left side, flattening the view. Lymph nodes between the aorta and inferior vena cava are sampled and placed in a bag. Visible structures include the inferior vena cava, left renal vein, and aorta.

The surgeon continues mobilizing the duodenum along the left renal vein, pulling it through from the opposite side. A visible window has developed, allowing the duodenum to be pulled through. The surgeon releases bloodless attachments around the duodenal jejunal flexure, also known as the ligament of Treitz.

The surgeon opens the bowel and divides it, creating an incision in the mesentery, which is the fatty tissue containing the bowel's blood supply. An energy device is used to seal and cut the tissue, allowing the surgeon to progress along the mesentery.

The surgeon returns to the left side, with the duodenum pulled across, revealing the head of the pancreas above the instrument. A small vein from the pancreas to the superior mesenteric vein is visible and divided. The bowel, appearing purple due to loss of blood supply, is pulled through and divided at the pink section using a stapling device. The duodenum and pancreas are lifted away from the vessels, increasing mobility. A small tributary of the superior mesenteric vein is divided.

The surgeon dissects along the artery, taking branches from it, including one large main branch and several smaller ones. The uncinate process of the pancreas is lifted off the mesentery of the bowel. The head of the pancreas is elevated, and the neck of the pancreas is divided at the point where it runs in front of the vein.

The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to carefully seal off small blood vessels within the vascular pancreas. Upon reaching the pancreatic duct, scissors are used to divide the duct, revealing clear pancreatic juice. The remaining pancreatic tissue is then divided using the harmonic scalpel.

The surgeon cuts a small piece from the edge of the pancreatic duct specimen for a frozen section analysis. The dissection continues along the artery and in front of the vein, with visible manipulation of the surrounding tissue.

The surgeon clips and divides the inferior pancreaticoduodenal artery and tributaries running into the superior mesenteric vein and portal vein. The surgical field is cleared of the superior mesenteric artery, and lymphatic tissue is removed from behind the portal vein. A suction device is used near the hepatic artery, with the gastroduodenal artery clipped.

The surgeon retracts the vein using a sucker to access and remove the remaining lymph nodes. The inferior vena cava, staple bile duct, gastroduodenal artery, and portal vein are visible. The specimen is placed in a plastic bag for later removal, and the staple line is cut away, revealing a healthy blood supply to the bile duct. The lower bile duct is prepared for pathological examination.

A temporary clamp is placed across the blood supply of the bile duct to facilitate the anastomosis. The bowel is brought through in preparation for joining the bile duct and pancreatic duct. A small hole is made in the bowel, gently opened with forceps, and the back wall is stitched first, followed by the front wall, ensuring clear visibility of each stitch.

The surgeon carefully sews the bile duct and pancreas duct joins, completing the back wall stitching. The suture is brought out but not tied yet, as it will be tied to the front wall stitch once that is completed.
The assistant follows the stitch closely to maintain tension and prevent snags or loose sutures. The surgeon completes the front wall stitching and ties it to the back wall stitch, securing the join.
The surgeon begins the pancreas join, performing it in two layers to minimize the risk of leakage, which could lead to infections. The join is carefully executed to ensure a secure connection.

The surgeon inserts a plastic tube or stent into the pancreas duct to aid in manipulating the pancreas during stitching, preventing accidental blockage of the duct by stitches. The pancreas appears highly vascular, with bleeding stopping as the stitch is pulled through.

The surgeon performs a continuous suture along the back wall using a permanent stitch, creating a barrier. A small hole is opened in the bowel, similar to the bile duct join, and sutures are passed and placed to secure the join.

The surgeon performs the pancreatic duct anastomosis using interrupted dissolving sutures. Multiple sutures are placed around the relatively large pancreatic duct, with knots tied internally that will dissolve quickly.

The surgeon places a few sutures on the back wall and at the corners of the anastomosis site. A stent is passed from the pancreatic duct into the bowel side to ensure a secure closure without narrowing the join.
The surgeon places front stitches in the pancreatic anastomosis without tying them, preparing for the next step in securing the join.
The prepared length of bowel is positioned and ready to be joined to the stomach, indicating the next step in the surgical procedure.

The surgeon creates a small hole in the stomach and bowel to insert a stapler, which places staples on either side of a knife, forming a vertical line for the posterior join between the bowel and the stomach. The visible white line indicates the stapled join, and the remaining hole at the front is subsequently closed.

The surgeon places a corner stitch on one side of the anastomosis, followed by a stitch from the opposite side. A new stitch with barbs is then passed into the tissue, which locks in position without the need for knot tying, preventing suture loosening.

The surgeon continues stitching towards the previously placed corner stitch, incorporating the staple line into the sutures to ensure inclusion of healthy stomach tissue with adequate blood supply. Once the stitching is complete, the sutures are cut.

The surgeon removes the gallbladder, which was previously used to lift the liver during the bile duct anastomosis. The gallbladder is placed in a retrieval bag. Drains are positioned around the surgical joins to remove any residual blood or fluid, followed by irrigation of the abdominal cavity.
